title: Colonization of Kazakhstan by the Russian Empire (18th–19th centuries)
overview: Lecture 4 follows the gradual transformation of the Kazakh Khanate into a colonial outskirt of the Russian Empire. It begins with the Kazakh-Jungar wars of the early 18th century that drove the Junior Zhuz to seek Russian protection (1731), traces the Cossack-military line of fortresses, the abolition of the khans' power under the Charters of 1822 (Speransky, Middle Zhuz) and 1824 (Orenburg, Junior Zhuz), the 1867–1868 administrative reforms creating three Governor-Generalships, and the Stolypin resettlement policy that transferred tens of millions of desyatinas of Kazakh pasture to Slavic peasant colonists by 1917.

coreConcepts.definition: 'Years of Great Disaster' (1723) – the catastrophic Jungar invasion when the Kazakhs fled, exhausted to the point of 'walking until soles turned white', to lakes Alakol; tens of thousands of captives and herds were carried off.
coreConcepts.example: Pushed the Junior Zhuz of Abulkhair toward seeking Russian military protection.

coreConcepts.definition: Oirat (Western Mongol) state formed in 1635 from four large tribes: Khoshout, Torgout, Derbet and Choros. Used heavy cavalry charges against the lighter Kazakh cavalry.
coreConcepts.example: The most aggressive Jungar leader was Khuntaiji Batur in the mid-17th century.

coreConcepts.definition: Junior Zhuz khan Abulkhair (r. 1710–1748) requested Russian subjecthood; on 10 October 1731 Empress Anna Ioannovna signed the letter of acceptance.
coreConcepts.example: Both sides understood 'subjecthood' differently – Abulkhair wanted military aid and protection from Nadir Shah and Jungars; Russia treated it as the start of imperial annexation.

coreConcepts.definition: Chains of fortresses and outposts built by Russia along the steppe frontier with Yaitsky, Orenburg, Siberian and Novo-Ishim Cossack hosts.
coreConcepts.example: Orsk (1735), Orenburg (1735, capital of Orenburg Province 1744), Orenburg Cossack Host (1748); by 1745 the Yaitsky Cossacks had 7 fortresses and 11 outposts; by 1769 about 15,000 Cossack families lived along the Yaitskaya line. In 1752 the Novo-Ishimskaya line linked Omsk with Zverinogolovskaya (Baglan) with 10 fortresses and 53 redoubts.

coreConcepts.definition: Reform drafted by M.M. Speransky, Governor-General of Siberia, abolishing the khan's power in the Middle Zhuz. The steppe was split into okrugs (districts) of 15–20 volosts headed by Agha-Sultans ('senior sultans'); each volost had 10–12 auls.
coreConcepts.example: First okrugs opened: Karkaraly and Kokchetav (1824), then Ayaguz (1831), Akmola (1832), Bayanaul and Ush-Bulak (1833), Amankaragai (1834), Kokpekti (1844).

coreConcepts.definition: Drafted by the Orenburg Governor-General, approved by the Asian Committee in 1824; abolished the khan's power in the Junior Zhuz. Every newly elected Agha-Sultan had to publicly reaffirm an oath of loyalty to the Russian emperor.
coreConcepts.example: Junior Zhuz divided into three parts (Eastern, Middle, Western).

coreConcepts.definition: Two 'Temporary Regulations' that reorganized all of Kazakhstan into 3 Governor-Generalships (Turkestan, Orenburg, West Siberian/Steppe), 6 oblasts, then uezds → volosts → auls. Land was declared state property.
coreConcepts.example: 11 July 1867 – Regulation on Semirechensk and Syrdarya regions; 21 October 1868 – Temporary Regulation on Orenburg and West Siberian steppe.

coreConcepts.definition: After the 1861 abolition of serfdom in Russia, landless peasants migrated en masse to Siberia and the Kazakh steppe; the Shcherbina Expedition (1896 onward) calculated 'surplus' pasture for redistribution.
coreConcepts.example: By 1905 more than 4 million desyatinas of Kazakh pasture had been earmarked for resettlement; the final figure was set at 18 million desyatinas.

expandedNotes.heading: The Jungar Wars (1723–1758)
expandedNotes.body: Having made peace with the Qing, the Jungars invaded the Kazakh steppes in early spring 1723. Scattered Kazakh detachments offered little resistance; the survivors fled to Lake Alakol – the trauma remembered as 'Aktaban shubyryndy' ('walking until soles turned white'). The Kazakhs lost large parts of the Senior and Middle Zhuz, including Turkestan, the khans' residence. Kazakh counter-victories came at the Battle of Bulanty (1727) and the Anrakay Battle (1729 or 1730) under the leadership of Abulkhair. Heavy losses meant the danger of new Jungar attacks remained. In autumn 1739 Jungars resumed invasions; in February 1741 a 30,000-strong army led by Galdan-Tseren's eldest son Lama-Dorji reached the Tobol and Ishim. In 1741 Sultan Abylai was captured with 200 men. The struggle produced a galaxy of batyrs: Bogembay, Kabanbay, Malaisary, Zhanybek, Bayan, Iset, Baigozy, Zhatay, Urazymbet, Tursynbay, Raiymbek and others; Abylai stands out as the leader of the batyr movement. In spring 1755 a huge Qing army invaded Jungaria, capturing Khan Davatsi and bringing him to Beijing; by 1758 Jungaria was destroyed and the Qing absorbed Xinjiang.

expandedNotes.heading: Abulkhair and the Acceptance of Russian Subjecthood (1731)
expandedNotes.body: Abulkhair khan (r. 1710–1748), khan with influence over the Junior Zhuz, consolidated power during the 'barefoot retreat' caused by the Jungar invasion of 1723–1725. According to scholar Noda Jin, Abulkhair maneuvered between the Russians and Nadir Shah of Persia for his own benefit. He petitioned for Russian subjecthood; on 10 October 1731 Empress Anna Ioannovna signed the letter of acceptance. The two parties understood the act very differently: Abulkhair counted on Russian protection and a military-aid exchange; the Russian Empire saw the steppes as a future imperial outskirt (I.V. Yerofeyeva 1999, Jin Noda 2016). Russian subjecthood was at this stage formal and superficial. In 1740 Abilmambet and Abylai of the Middle Zhuz also swore allegiance to the Russian empress. According to Noda, Abylai 'maneuvered between the Chinese god-khan of the Qing Empire and the Russian Empire'; he tried to remain as independent as possible by exploiting the fact that both empires lacked complete information about each other's steppe policy.

expandedNotes.heading: Three Pillars of Russian Colonial Policy
expandedNotes.body:
Tsarism gradually eliminated Kazakh political autonomy through three coordinated lines:
1) MILITARY consolidation – construction of fortified lines saturated with garrisons; permanent Cossack settlements alongside them.
2) POLITICAL consolidation – administrative reforms creating okrugs, then later a 'distance' system, sharply limiting Kazakh political rights.
3) ECONOMIC consolidation – mass seizure of Kazakh lands, taxation, and monopolies on fishing, logging, etc.

expandedNotes.heading: Cossack Lines
expandedNotes.body: From the start of acceptance into Russian subjecthood, Russia pursued the steady annexation of the entire Kazakh steppe. In 1735 Orenburg was founded; in 1744 it became the centre of Orenburg Province; in 1748 the Orenburg Cossack Host was set up, organized close to regular troops and serving the Verkhneyaitskaya line from Yaitsky town to the Verkhneyaitskaya fortress. The Yaitsky Cossacks settled along the Zhayik (Ural) River – 7 fortresses, 11 outposts by 1745; ~15,000 families by 1769. In 1740–1743 fortresses were built at the junction of the Junior Zhuz and Southern Urals. In 1752 the Novo-Ishimskaya line was built – 10 fortresses, 53 redoubts – linking the Uiskaya and Irtyshskaya lines and connecting Omsk with Zverinogolovskaya. Cossack lines became the spine of military-political control over the steppe.

expandedNotes.heading: Charters of 1822 (Speransky) and 1824
expandedNotes.body:
Under M.M. Speransky, Governor-General of Siberia, the 'Charter on the Siberian Kirghiz' (22 June 1822) divided Siberia into Eastern (Irkutsk) and Western (Tobolsk, from 1839 Omsk) regions. The Middle Zhuz steppe became 'Region of the Siberian Kirghiz' inside Western Siberia. Each okrug had 15–20 volosts headed by Agha-Sultans; each volost held 10–12 auls. The Junior Zhuz received a parallel Charter in 1824 (drafted by the Orenburg Governor-General, approved by the Asian Committee). The khan's power was abolished in both zhuzes. Newly elected Agha-Sultans had to publicly reaffirm an oath of fealty to the Russian emperor.
ADMINISTRATIVE PYRAMID (Western Siberia): Governor-General → Chief of Omsk → Delegates (zasedatel') + Agha-Sultan → Volost Sultan.
First okrugs: Karkaraly and Kokchetav (1824); Ayaguz (1831); Akmola (1832); Bayanaul and Ush-Bulak (1833); Amankaragai (1834); Kokpekti (1844).

expandedNotes.heading: Kazakh Resistance to 1822/1824 Reforms
expandedNotes.body: Some Kazakh sultans tried to continue the previous policy of Abylai by seeking the title of khan from the Qing emperor. Wali Khan's son Gubaidulla wrote to the Chinese bogdykhan asking for the title of khan. According to Noda, the Qing court failed to perceive the introduction of the 1822 Regulations and the diplomatic implications. Russia, citing the 1822 Charter, notified Gubaidulla that accepting a khan title from the Qing was an illegal act – the first time Russia interfered in steppe matters to bar a Chinese-conferred khanship.

expandedNotes.heading: Reforms of 1867–1868
expandedNotes.body: The main task of the 1860s–1890s reforms in Russia was 'to achieve a merger of the Kirghiz steppes with other parts of Russia.' Drivers: intensifying colonization and the development of rich raw-material sources. A project was drafted in 1865–1866. On 11 July 1867 the 'Regulations on the Administration in Semirechensk and Syrdarya Regions' were adopted; on 21 October 1868 the 'Temporary Regulations on the Administration of the Steppe Regions of the Orenburg and West Siberian Governor-Generalship.' The entire territory of Kazakhstan was divided into 3 Governor-Generalships – Turkestan, Orenburg, West Siberian. They split into oblasts, then uezds, volosts and auls (the same hierarchy as the rest of the empire). Local Kazakhs could only be elected to the lowest levels (volost and aul). The traditional system of government was thereby eliminated, opening the way to mass settler colonization and displacement of nomads to unsuitable lands.

expandedNotes.heading: Resettlement Policy
expandedNotes.body: After the 1861 emancipation of the serfs in Russia, vast numbers of landless peasants migrated to Siberia and the Far East; the Kazakh steppes became a major direction of spontaneous migration. The Shcherbina Expedition – funded by the Ministry of Agriculture and State Properties – was the colonial project tasked with finding lands for resettlement. Shcherbina used a two-step method: (1) identify communes on winter pastures, count farms and livestock to calculate the needs of an 'average household'; (2) determine 'surplus' rangeland that could be cut from the commune. The Atbasar uezd map of 1897 shows the method in action. By 1905 over 4 million desyatinas of Kazakh land had been earmarked for resettlement; the final figure was set at 18 million desyatinas. Under Stolypin's 1906 agrarian reform, the flow accelerated dramatically – about 1.5 million peasants moved to Kazakhstan by 1917, producing social pressure that erupted in the 1916 uprising.

expandedNotes.heading: Outcome: Loss of Independence
expandedNotes.body: Despite withstanding Jungar aggression, the Kazakh Khanate faced new threats from two strengthening empires on its borders – Russia and China. Russia advanced through military-Cossack colonization, administrative reforms and land colonization, which together ended Kazakh political independence by the end of the 19th century. The traditional system was destroyed; pastures were cut for settlers; numerous taxes and a colonial fishing/logging monopoly were imposed. This loss of land and identity provoked the anti-colonial movements (Syrym, Isatay-Makhambet, Kenesary) and ultimately the Alash movement (Lecture 6).

keyScholars.name: Abulkhair Khan
keyScholars.contribution: Khan of the Junior Zhuz; led Kazakh victories at Bulanty (1727) and Anrakay (1729); initiated Russian subjecthood in 1731.

keyScholars.name: Empress Anna Ioannovna
keyScholars.contribution: Signed the 10 October 1731 letter of acceptance of the Junior Zhuz into Russian subjecthood.

keyScholars.name: Abylai Khan
keyScholars.contribution: Khan of the Middle Zhuz (1771–1781) and leader of the batyr movement; maneuvered between Russia and Qing China to preserve Kazakh independence.

keyScholars.name: Khuntaiji Batur
keyScholars.contribution: Most aggressive ruler of the Jungar Khanate; consolidated Oirat tribes for war on the Kazakhs.

keyScholars.name: Mikhail M. Speransky
keyScholars.contribution: Russian statesman and Governor-General of Siberia; author of the 1822 Charter on the Siberian Kirghiz.

keyScholars.name: Gubaidulla Sultan
keyScholars.contribution: Son of Wali Khan; sought the title of khan from the Qing emperor – became the test case for Russia's barring of foreign-conferred khanships.

keyScholars.name: Fedor Shcherbina
keyScholars.contribution: Russian statistician; led the Shcherbina Expedition (from 1896) that calculated 'surplus' Kazakh pastures for settler resettlement.

keyScholars.name: Pyotr Stolypin
keyScholars.contribution: Russian Prime Minister whose 1906 agrarian reform launched mass peasant resettlement to Kazakhstan.

comparativeTables.title: Stages of Russian Annexation of the Kazakh Steppe
rows: Abulkhair → Anna Ioannovna; threat of Jungars
Conquest of Southern Kazakhstan; capture of Chimkent and Aulie-Ata (1864)

comparativeTables.title: Key Cossack Hosts on the Steppe Frontier

realWorldExamples: Orenburg, founded 1735, remained the capital of the Kazakh ASSR until 1925 – almost two centuries of role as the gateway to the steppe.
The two Lake Alakol – on the path of the 1723 Kazakh retreat – became symbols of Aktaban shubyryndy in Kazakh oral memory.
The Bulanty (1727) and Anrakay (1729/1730) battlefields are now memorial sites; the slogan 'Алқакөл сұлама' commemorates the survivors.
Cossack station names (Verkhneyaitskaya, Pres-novsk, Petropavlovsk, Semipalatinsk, Ust-Kamenogorsk) still mark the historic Cossack lines on modern maps.
Stolypin agrarian reform villages (such as Mariinsky and Pokrovsky) in northern Kazakhstan remain in place today – legacy of the 1.5 million-strong peasant migration.

timeline.event: Formation of the Jungar Khanate (Oirat tribes united)
Jungar invasion: 'Aktaban shubyryndy' – Years of Great Disaster
Kazakh victory at the Battle of Bulanty under Abulkhair
Kazakh victory at Anrakay (some sources 1730)
Anna Ioannovna signs the letter of acceptance of the Junior Zhuz
Foundation of Orenburg
Abilmambet and Abylai of the Middle Zhuz swear allegiance
Orenburg Cossack Host organized
Novo-Ishimskaya fortified line built (10 forts, 53 redoubts)
Qing invasion destroys the Jungar Khanate
Jungaria absorbed into Qing Xinjiang
Speransky's Charter on the Siberian Kirghiz (Middle Zhuz)
Charter on the Orenburg Kirghiz (Junior Zhuz)
Senior Zhuz brought largely under Russian rule
Abolition of serfdom in Russia – start of peasant migration east
Russian capture of Chimkent and Aulie-Ata
Regulations on Semirechensk and Syrdarya Regions
Temporary Regulations on Orenburg and West Siberian steppe
Shcherbina Expedition completes Atbasar uezd map
Stolypin agrarian reform accelerates resettlement

mustMemorize: 1635 – Jungar Khanate formed (4 Oirat tribes: Khoshout, Torgout, Derbet, Choros)
1727 Bulanty + 1729/1730 Anrakay – Kazakh victories under Abulkhair
10 October 1731 – Junior Zhuz becomes Russian subjects (Abulkhair / Anna Ioannovna)
1822 – Speransky's Charter on the Siberian Kirghiz (Middle Zhuz)
11 July 1867 + 21 October 1868 – three Governor-Generalships, six oblasts
Shcherbina Expedition – 18 million desyatinas earmarked for resettlement
Stolypin 1906 – mass migration, ~1.5 million peasants by 1917
cheatSheet:
1635 Jungars → 1723 Aktaban shubyryndy → 1727 Bulanty / 1729 Anrakay → 1731 Junior (Abulkhair + Anna Ioannovna) → 1740 Middle → 1822 Speransky (M. Zhuz) / 1824 (J. Zhuz) → 1847 Senior → 1867/1868 3 Governor-Generalships → 1906 Stolypin
Key hosts: Yaitsky, Orenburg, Siberian, Novo-Ishim
Admin pyramid: Gov-Gen → Oblast → Uezd → Volost → Aul
mnemonics: 22-24: Sper-ansky Siberian, Or-enburg Charters (numbers in dates)
67-68: SIX oblasts in TWO Governor-Generalships years
ABA-A: Aktaban → Bulanty → Anrakay → Abulkhair (sequence of 1720s events)
S-O-S Cossack hosts: Siberian, Orenburg, Semirechensk on the steppe frontier
